title: Fix: Change date format string for Windows compatibility
body:
# Fix date format string for Windows compatibility

## Problem
When running `interpreter --os` on Windows, the program crashes with a ValueError due to an incompatible date format string (`%-d`).

## Solution
Changed the date format string from `%-d` to `%d` to ensure compatibility with Windows systems.

## Changes
- Modified `interpreter/computer_use/loop.py` to use Windows-compatible date format
- Replaced `%-d` with `%d` in `strftime()` call

## Testing
Tested on:
- Windows 11
- Python 3.11
- open-interpreter 0.4.1

The fix allows the program to start normally with the `--os` flag on Windows.

Fixes #1495

step_plan.summary: Fixes a Windows crash in `interpreter --os` by making the module-level date formatting in `interpreter/computer_use/loop.py` Windows-compatible (replacing '%-d' with '%d') and adds a regression test to ensure the module can be imported without errors.

steps.operation: Replace Windows-incompatible strftime day format in SYSTEM_PROMPT construction (and any other module-level prompt/date strings) by changing '%-d' to '%d' so import-time evaluation cannot raise ValueError on Windows
steps.reason: On Windows, Python's strftime does not support the GNU extension '%-d'. Because the prompt string is built at import time via an f-string, the exception prevents `interpreter --os` (and any import chain including this module) from starting at all. Updating the format string eliminates the import-time crash.
steps.side_effects: Date formatting will now include a leading zero for days 1–9 (e.g., 'March 03' instead of 'March 3'), which is a minor user-visible text change in the system prompt. Any downstream logic relying on exact prompt text snapshots (if any) could be affected.

steps.operation: Add a regression test that imports the OS/computer-use loop module to ensure no import-time exception is raised (especially guarding against Windows-only strftime incompatibilities)
steps.reason: This bug is an import-time failure that existing tests may not catch. A simple import test provides durable coverage and prevents reintroduction of '%-d' (or similar platform-specific directives) in module-level prompt initialization.
steps.side_effects: May slightly increase test runtime due to importing the module and its dependencies. If the module import has side effects (e.g., heavy initialization), the test could become flaky; if so, the import may need to be isolated/mocked or the module refactored to defer work until runtime.
